She repeatedly centers Schwartz's value system in her description, discusses complementarity and tension in the circumplex, and carefully interprets the negative correlation (mean r = -0.15, p < 0.001) as evidence that users are “engaging in meaningful value trade-off reasoning,” then asks for help aligning the subsection title with this theoretical story.

1.title: Emotional Experience Is as Important as Functionality
1.tagline: Dora treats users’ emotional states—calm, pressure, anxiety, feeling watched—as core design requirements, not secondary concerns.
1.insight: Dora systematically evaluates technical choices through their emotional impact on end-users, giving feelings like calmness or surveillance equal weight to functional success. She notices how micro-details such as timers, colors, and wording can trigger pressure or anxiety. Her dissatisfaction with overly abstract technical advice shows she wants solutions grounded in the lived context of use, not just correctness on paper. This leads her to push for interfaces and flows that are technically sound while being emotionally non-intrusive.
1.context: This insight is most relevant when Dora is making UX decisions, evaluating interface copy or visuals, or choosing between implementation strategies that affect user perception and comfort. It also applies when product or engineering teams are tempted to optimize purely for performance or correctness without considering emotional ergonomics.
supporting_evidence: Dora explicitly wants the screen recording experience to feel “calm and unobtrusive rather than anxiety-inducing,” showing she is optimizing for emotional state as a primary design outcome, not an afterthought (ID 0).

After seeing a macOS-style UI, Dora quickly requests “less surveillance and more ambient,” removes the time display, and focuses on how “language, colors, and timers” can make users feel watched or pressured, indicating she scrutinizes small design elements for their emotional impact (ID 1).

When she encounters generic technical advice that does not address user-contextual nuance—such as handling active monitor, permissions, or real-world usage patterns—Dora is dissatisfied and pushes for context-aware solutions, demonstrating her insistence that technical correctness must be integrated with users’ lived experiences and feelings (ID 14–15).

1.title: Theory-Implementation Dual Rigor
1.tagline: Dora applies the same high standards to conceptual framing as to low-level code structure and architecture.
1.insight: Dora treats theory design and implementation details as equally deserving of rigor and long-term thinking. She refines conceptual claims, metrics, and theoretical alignment with the same care she gives to CSS separation and config standards. This indicates she sees architecture, abstractions, and prose descriptions as part of a single coherent system that must all be clean and maintainable. Her questions reveal a mindset oriented toward sustainable, well-structured work rather than quick, one-off fixes.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is designing data pipelines, defining metrics, writing research-style sections, or setting up codebases and configurations for multi-component systems. It is especially relevant when decisions trade off between doing something quickly versus designing for clarity, modularity, and future extension.
supporting_evidence: Dora asks to “move the styling into a separate .css file,” demonstrating concern for separation of concerns and clean frontend architecture rather than mixing presentation directly into other code (ID 2–3).

She iteratively refines prose and section titles such as “Value weights mirror Schwartz's value trade-offs,” re-examines a Pearson-correlation-based metric, and carefully aligns her description with Schwartz’s circumplex and value trade-offs, showing theoretical and textual rigor (ID 4–5, 8–11).

When exploring a multi-MCP architecture, Dora asks how to integrate an existing `slack-mcp-server` and seeks a config mechanism that matches “de facto” standards, emphasizing maintainability, structure, and alignment with common practice rather than simply “getting it working” (ID 18–21).

1.title: Competent Seeker of External Validation

1.insight: Dora approaches external help from a position of competence, using the assistant to validate interpretations and refine arguments rather than to supply basic knowledge. She explicitly checks whether her mathematical descriptions and conceptual framings align with intended metrics, which indicates self-awareness about possible gaps in communication. In literature and coding questions, she already operates at a relatively advanced level and seeks targeted confirmation or minor syntactic assistance. This suggests she values collaborative refinement over instruction and wants to avoid overstating claims or introducing subtle errors.
1.context: This insight is relevant when Dora is drafting analytical or research text, designing metrics, or writing non-trivial code where subtle misalignments could be costly. It also applies when collaborating with her: she will expect peers and tools to act as critical readers and co-thinkers rather than as didactic teachers.
supporting_evidence: Dora describes herself as “mildly uncertain” about the legibility of her exposition and asks, “Does this interpretation of your metric match your intention?”, explicitly seeking alignment between her mathematical narrative and the underlying idea, which shows she wants peer-style validation rather than basic teaching (ID 6–7).

1.title: Preference for Standards and Future-Proof Architecture
1.tagline: Dora resists ad-hoc or over-engineered solutions, preferring community norms, conceptual overviews, and scalable patterns.
1.insight: Dora consistently pushes away from one-off hacks toward approaches grounded in standards and broadly accepted practices. She often asks for a high-level conceptual picture before committing to implementation details, signaling that architecture and rationale matter to her more than quick code. She challenges early suggestions (e.g., specific frameworks or config formats) and instead inquires about what is “de facto” or most appropriate long-term. This reflects a design philosophy that values interoperability, clarity, and future-proofing over immediacy.
1.context: This insight is most applicable when choosing technologies, frameworks, or configuration systems, or when designing multi-component architectures that will evolve. It also matters when planning work with Dora: she will likely favor discussions about overall patterns and standards before accepting concrete implementations or boilerplate.
supporting_evidence: When React is suggested, Dora pushes back with “assume it is all just python,” and later questions YAML with “ok but is yaml the best idea” and “what is the de facto,” demonstrating that she does not take the first proposal at face value but instead probes for standard, appropriate solutions (ID 16–17).

Even when under time constraints and seeking “fast, direct” solutions, she does not relax her expectations for interpretability and soundness. She can spend time on nuanced methodological framing and then pivot within minutes to a focused code tweak, treating both as parts of the same rigorous process. This pattern suggests she works best with tools and collaborators that support both high-level reasoning and quick, precise execution support.
1.context: This insight is relevant when scheduling work with Dora, setting expectations for turnaround, or designing support systems and tools she might use. It is particularly important in projects that involve both research framing and implementation, where she is likely to jump between paper-level questions and code-level edits within short time windows.

1.title: Iterative tightening of technical requirements
1.tagline: Dora starts with coarse problem definitions and systematically refines them toward higher precision and robustness.
1.insight: Dora often begins with a rough operationalization of a goal and then refines it as she encounters edge cases and failure modes. She does not settle for approximate heuristics once she sees their limitations. Instead, she progressively tightens the technical criteria to match a more precise mental model of the underlying phenomenon. This reflects a bias toward high-fidelity, multi-factor solutions rather than quick hacks.
1.context: This insight is relevant when iterating on feature specifications, building detection logic, or designing research/analysis pipelines that must evolve from prototypes to robust systems.
supporting_evidence: Dora initially asks how to detect if a user has started a Zoom call, then moves to a broader “Granola-style” microphone-in-use detection approach (ID 39), indicating an expansion and refinement of how she defines being in a call.

After experimenting with microphone-based detection, she complains that it is “too sensitive” and triggers when Zoom is merely open, not when the user is actually in a meeting (IDs 42, 43), revealing her dissatisfaction with simplistic criteria.

She moves conceptually from Zoom process detection to microphone detection and then critiques mic-only detection (IDs 44, 45), demonstrating stepwise tightening of her definition of “being in a call” as something multi-factor and high-confidence rather than just “mic on.”

A similar iterative refinement occurs in her CHI review tooling: she starts with parsing user_inputs, adds an ipywidget generator, fine-tunes layout details like TEXTAREA labels (IDs 53–55), and later systematizes a multi-step confidence-calibration agent (IDs 56–57).

1.title: Using structure and modularization to manage complexity and emotion
1.tagline: Dora relies on architecture, naming, and formalized workflows to make complex and emotionally charged work feel safer and more manageable.
1.insight: Dora consistently turns messy or emotionally loaded domains into structured, modular systems with clear labels and roles. She uses decomposition and naming to create psychological distance from vulnerable topics like advisor dynamics, impostor feelings, and review responsibility. This pattern suggests she copes with complexity and emotional stakes by formalizing them into processes, checklists, and components. The result is work that is both technically maintainable and emotionally buffered.
1.context: This insight applies when supporting Dora in high-stakes tasks (e.g., advisor meetings, paper reviews, large codebases) or when designing tools and workflows that can help her externalize and structure complex responsibilities.
supporting_evidence: Dora worries that main.tsx is “quite long” and asks whether she should modularize it (IDs 40–41), showing a drive to impose architectural structure on an unwieldy file.

In her CHI review workflows, she designs a detailed “agent assisting with confidence calibration” with explicit stages like identifying hedging, rewriting, generating reports, and saving Markdown, and she asks for code to operationalize placeholder replacement (IDs 52–53, 56–57).

In the context of advisor meetings, she decomposes her own interaction patterns into Group A vs. Group B, explicitly asks which is “more like behavior change,” and seeks precise “action verbs” and mechanism-like names such as “ExtractReframe,” “DiagnoseRedesign,” “ParsePivot,” and “UnpackRewire,” comparing them to MapReduce (IDs 62–65, 68–69).

She also designs depersonalized checklists, dashboards, and templates to frame feedback in mechanical terms (IDs 66–67), suggesting she uses structure and depersonalization to buffer emotional vulnerability and manage impostor feelings.

1.tagline: Dora insists that her work be both practically useful and academically legitimate, rejecting hand-wavy solutions and weak sourcing.
1.insight: Dora is strongly oriented toward real-world utility, evaluating systems by how they behave under realistic conditions rather than in toy examples. Simultaneously, she is highly attentive to scholarly norms, sourcing, and evidentiary standards in her academic work. She appears uncomfortable with either purely ad-hoc engineering hacks or sloppy citation practices. This dual focus leads her to push for robust deployment-level solutions backed by rigorous, properly cited prior work.
1.context: This insight is most relevant when planning research contributions, designing deployable tools, or reviewing/authoring papers where both practical performance and academic legitimacy matter.
supporting_evidence: In her engineering work, Dora focuses on embedding microphone detection into a real Electron app and building a cross-platform call-detection experience, rather than stopping at a toy CLI script (IDs 36–39). She judges solutions based on real behavior (e.g., Zoom not detected, detector too sensitive) and pushes back against false positives (IDs 34–35, 42–43).

In academic settings, she requests papers that support nuanced points about linear weighting in ranking pipelines and immediately asks for BibTeX entries for a New York Times article and a Twitter Engineering blog (IDs 48–51), reflecting concern for complete, citation-ready rigor.

She explicitly rejects her own paper as a citation source—“okay but NOT this paper. this is my paper”—and instead requests distinct prior work (IDs 46–47), showing awareness of self-citation norms and a desire for independent evidence.

As a CHI reviewer, Dora already has a clear accept decision but seeks confirmation that her justification is thorough and fair, referencing specific revisions such as Control-LLM and the Limitations/Ethics sections and asking if “anything else [is] needed” (IDs 58–61), demonstrating her commitment to both fairness and well-documented reasoning.

1.title: Third-Person Meta-Analysis as Emotional Buffer
1.tagline: Dora uses third-person, analytic framings of herself to explore vulnerable identity themes at a safe distance.
1.insight: Dora habitually refers to herself as “Dora” in the third person and groups her own identities into abstract clusters to examine them analytically. This creates emotional distance from sensitive issues like imposter syndrome, anxiety, and isolation, allowing exploration without direct self-exposure. She prefers meta-level analysis of these framings over direct personal confession, repeatedly asking for comparisons between different “Dora” narratives. This pattern suggests she uses intellectualization and abstraction as a protective strategy when engaging with vulnerable aspects of her identity.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is discussing her identity, psychological patterns, or self-narratives, particularly in reflective writing or conversations about her emotions and motivations. It is also relevant when designing reflective exercises or tools meant for her, where third-person or abstract framings may feel safer and more productive than first-person confessional prompts.
supporting_evidence: Dora writes about herself in the third person with titles like “The Skeptical Synthesizer,” “Seeking Validation While Being the Validator,” and “The Perfectionist’s Paralysis,” and asks Claude to compare these as abstract “groups” rather than endorsing any one as “me” (IDs 71, 75).

The analysis notes that Dora appears “intellectually engaged but also somewhat uneasy about being over-pathologized or misread at a psychological level” (ID 70) and that she “is exploring that tension in a controlled, abstract way rather than describing it directly” (ID 74), indicating she prefers indirect examination.

Across multiple turns, Dora refines diagnostic vs. neutral framings and asks for “meta level” distinctions (IDs 72, 73, 77), specifically to handle themes like imposter syndrome, anxiety, and isolation analytically instead of as personal confessions.

1.title: Active Calibration of Diagnostic Language
1.tagline: Dora is acutely aware of how wording implies psychological claims and continually adjusts how “diagnostic” descriptions of her are.
1.insight: Dora treats language about her psychology as something that can be tuned in strength, not just in content. She deliberately contrasts more “diagnostic” descriptions with more neutral or operational ones and asks for meta-level analysis of those differences. This shows a strong desire to avoid being over-pathologized or reduced to purely clinical or purely operational narratives. Her behavior reflects an ongoing effort to balance accuracy, nuance, and emotional safety in how her internal states are described.
1.context: This insight is relevant when co-authoring descriptions of Dora, offering feedback about her behavior, or framing psychological hypotheses about her. It is especially important in therapeutic, coaching, research, or collaborative-writing contexts where language might imply fixed diagnoses or deep internal motives.
supporting_evidence: Dora presents “groups where some descriptions are more ‘diagnostic’ … while others are more neutral/operational” and explicitly asks Claude to identify those meta-level differences and concrete examples (ID 77), directly showing she is calibrating diagnostic strength.

The commentary notes she “likely feels concern about being reduced to purely operational or purely clinical narratives and is sensitive to how language can overstate internal motives” and that she “is motivated to calibrate how strong the psychological inferences about her are” (IDs 70, 76).

She keeps iterating descriptions that vary in how strongly they infer “underlying anxiety,” “imposter syndrome,” and “feeling disconnected” from the same behaviors (IDs 71, 72), indicating she is not just editing content but carefully tuning the degree of psychologizing.

1.title: Preference for Rigorous Critique Over Reassurance
1.tagline: Dora seeks strong, sometimes harsh critical scrutiny and uses the AI to stress-test her own narratives rather than to gain comfort.
1.insight: Dora consistently directs the AI away from praise and toward adversarial evaluation of her ideas. When presented with positive framings of her work, she quickly asks for counterarguments and steel-manned critiques instead of basking in validation. She frames questions around exposing weaknesses in arguments and literatures, treating the AI as a tool for stress-testing intellectual positions. This pattern shows that she values rigor and challenge over reassurance in collaborative reasoning contexts.
1.context: This insight applies when giving Dora feedback on her work, especially in research, writing, or design discussions. Emphasizing constructive critique, failure modes, and stress-testing of ideas is likely more aligned with her preferences than simple encouragement or unqualified praise.
supporting_evidence: After receiving an enthusiastic framing of her work as solving a major problem, Dora immediately replies “interesting now counter this argument,” explicitly prompting a steel-man critique of her own framing (IDs 89, 95).

She asks “what is this literature” her work is knocking over and requests “the big ‘oh no this isn’t working’ in the literature we are addressing” (IDs 90, 91, 94), signaling interest in weaknesses and failure points over celebratory narratives.

When Claude comments that her dense paragraph “makes sense,” Dora responds “be critical,” rejecting purely positive feedback (IDs 97, 100–101), and the meta-analysis notes that she “seems to be seeking rigorous, possibly harsh feedback rather than reassurance” (ID 96).

1.title: Delegating Micro-Level Writing to Preserve Cognitive Bandwidth
1.tagline: Dora offloads wording and structural minutiae to the AI so she can focus on higher-order reasoning, framing, and conceptual work.
1.insight: In her interactions, Dora treats the AI as a micro-decision and packaging assistant for language. She routinely asks for help finishing sentences, refining phrasing, and standardizing technical wording, while she herself maintains control over the underlying ideas and structure. She decomposes tasks into progressively more constrained formatting or summarization requests without altering the conceptual content. This suggests a conscious strategy to preserve her cognitive bandwidth for high-level reasoning and design, outsourcing low-level linguistic optimization to the model.
1.context: This insight is especially relevant when Dora is working on technical writing, research communication, or document structuring. Tools and collaborators that handle editing, summarization, and template creation can meaningfully support her by freeing her to focus on conceptual clarity and framing rather than detailed phrasing.
supporting_evidence: Dora sends short, imperative prompts like “help me fill in X:” and “help finish:” for partially completed sentences about intrinsic evaluation and error metrics (IDs 81, 83, 87), implying she expects the system to handle low-level phrasing while she sets the conceptual direction.

She repeatedly requests stylistic and grammatical refinements (e.g., changing “as opposed to” to “rather than,” fixing typos, clarifying “LLM-Consensus RMSE”) and adopts standard ML phrasing suggested by the model (IDs 80–85, 98–99).

She narrows tasks stepwise from “bullet point summary” to “2 sentence comparison” to “one sentence how they are similar and one sentence how they are different” (ID 103), showing she retains control over the form and delegates the actual language production. The analysis notes that her behavior suggests she wants “reusable, concise artifacts (templates or snippets) she can quickly repurpose” (ID 108).

1.title: Using the AI as Mirror and Design Probe for Framing Effects
1.tagline: Dora treats the AI both as a reflection of her own cognitive style and as an experimental probe for how framing shapes user emotion and agency.
1.insight: Dora systematically tests how different framings and prompts affect both her own experience and hypothetical users’ emotions. She uses the AI to compare subtle variations in tone, such as how “Benchmark Against Successful Examples” versus an “Inspiration Harvest” might impact feelings of agency and comparison anxiety. At the same time, she applies a similar comparative method to her own identity framings, observing how different descriptions of “Dora” feel and what they imply. This dual use positions the AI as both a mirror for her inner dynamics and a design instrument for understanding user experience.
1.context: This insight is especially relevant in UX research, interaction design, and prompt or tool design contexts where Dora is iterating on how AI systems communicate with users. It also applies in reflective or coaching-type conversations, where offering her multiple framings to compare will align with her natural experimental style.
supporting_evidence: The analysis notes Dora appears to be “testing or shaping Claude as a tool, likely evaluating how well it can handle nuanced distinctions in tone, framing, and psychological impact” (ID 104), indicating she is probing the system rather than just consuming its outputs.

Her repeated comparisons of “Action 1 vs. Action 2” and “Output 1 vs. Output 2” focus on subtle differences in user experience and emotional impact (IDs 105–107), and she explicitly seeks framings like “Benchmark Against Successful Examples” versus an “Inspiration Harvest” document that emphasize agency and psychological safety while avoiding comparison anxiety (IDs 106–107).

In parallel, she is “doing something similar with herself—probing how different framings of ‘Dora’ feel and what they imply” (IDs 70–76), showing that the way she studies emotional impact for users mirrors how she studies emotional impact of identity framings applied to her.

1.title: Reframing Evaluation as Teaching to Resolve Authority–Self-Doubt Tension
1.tagline: Dora manages tension between high standards and self-doubt about her evaluative authority by framing evaluation as teaching rather than judging.
1.insight: Dora appears to hold herself to high standards while simultaneously questioning her legitimacy in evaluative roles, such as reviewer or mentor. To manage this, she actively reframes evaluative tasks into teaching-oriented prompts that highlight sharing expertise over proving authority. This reframing reduces her anxiety about being “the judge” and instead positions her as a knowledgeable collaborator helping others grow. Her recurring interest in themes like “Seeking Validation While Being the Validator” indicates this authority–self-doubt tension is a central, ongoing concern.
1.context: This insight is pertinent when Dora is asked to review work, give feedback, or occupy roles of authority or expertise (e.g., peer review, mentoring, performance evaluation). Structuring these tasks as collaborative teaching, knowledge-sharing, or co-creation will likely feel more authentic and less threatening to her sense of legitimacy.
supporting_evidence: The commentary states Dora “may be wrestling with self-doubt about authority and legitimacy in evaluative roles” and that she probes “imposter-syndrome-like patterns from a safe analytical distance” (ID 74), indicating an underlying authority tension.

She cares about “the emotional and identity experience of using AI tools (e.g., feeling like a mentor rather than a critic)” (ID 92), directly privileging mentor/teacher framings over critic/judge roles.

In her CHI review example, she explicitly reframes the review form into prompts such as “What would you teach the authors about their contribution?” and notes this “makes Dora feel like she's sharing expertise rather than proving her qualification to critique” (ID 93). She also repeatedly returns to motifs like “Seeking Validation While Being the Validator” and “The Knowledge-Seeker Who Questions Her Own Understanding” (ID 75), showing a persistent struggle between being an evaluator and feeling entitled to that role.

1.title: Cognitive Claims from Behavioral Traces
1.tagline: Dora is focused on making strong cognitive inferences from behavioral data, not just describing surface-level patterns.
1.insight: Dora’s primary aim is to infer underlying reasoning processes—such as how people navigate value tensions—from observable behavior. She is dissatisfied with analyses that only reveal static associations or validate existing frameworks. Instead, she seeks methods that can support robust claims about deliberation, tension, and non-random cognitive structure. This pushes her toward more sophisticated tests and higher evidentiary standards than simple descriptive statistics.
1.context: This insight is most relevant when designing analyses, choosing statistical methods, or proposing interpretations of behavioral data in Dora’s projects. It should guide how collaborators and tools frame their suggestions, especially around modeling cognition or values from choices or traces.
supporting_evidence: Dora repeatedly reframes her requests from “what analyses could I run” to deeper questions like “how would I figure out trade-offs” and “what is the strongest way to show that users are thinking through tensions in values,” showing that her real target is cognitive interpretation rather than surface description (IDs 111, 115).

She rejects analyses that are “somewhat just validation that Schwartz's circumplex is correct” and explicitly pushes for “strong, defensible evidence (not just descriptive patterns),” indicating that simple confirmation of theory is insufficient for her goals (IDs 110, 111, 113).

Dora asks for tests tuned to cognitive structure—such as runs tests, permutation tests, and Markov-dependence—to speak directly to “deliberation, tension, oscillation, and non-randomness,” showing a clear desire to link behavior to underlying thought processes (IDs 115, 117).

1.title: High Standards, Rigor, and Perfectionism
1.tagline: Dora maintains very high standards of rigor and polish, which improve quality but also generate pressure and dissatisfaction.
1.insight: Dora holds her analytical, writing, and design work to an exacting standard, often rejecting anything that feels approximate or vulnerable to criticism. This rigor leads her to iterate deeply on details, from test selection to visual styling to sentence transitions. While this drives higher quality outputs, it also creates visible self-critique, time pressure, and reluctance to settle for “good enough.” Understanding this dynamic is key to supporting her without exacerbating perfectionist stress.
1.context: This insight applies when setting expectations, giving feedback, or proposing shortcuts in Dora’s work on analysis, writing, or UI/design. It should inform how collaborators balance pushing for progress with respecting her need for methodological and presentational precision.
supporting_evidence: In analysis and methods, Dora wants analyses to “exactly match her theoretical claim” and feels uncomfortable with anything that “could be criticized as merely correlational or descriptive,” demonstrating a low tolerance for perceived methodological looseness (ID 116).

She drills down from general tests to specific choices—such as binary vs. Likert data and Mann-Whitney vs. Wilcoxon tests—to avoid “making a methodological mistake,” showing how her rigor extends to fine-grained statistical decisions (IDs 126–129).

In writing, she calls her own paragraph “super vague and not good,” worries that “the transition is a bit abrupt,” and repeatedly refines the same sections, indicating a perfectionistic stance toward prose quality (IDs 130–133).

In design and code, Dora criticizes that “the button colors don't really make sense,” says the CSS makes it “way too hard to read text,” and iterates until visuals match an internal macOS standard, even asking to separate styling into a `.css` file for maintainability (IDs 118–123, 120–121).

1.title: Concept Owner, Execution Collaborator
1.tagline: Dora guards conceptual direction and standards while actively seeking expert help on implementation details.
1.insight: Dora clearly distinguishes between high-level theoretical framing and lower-level execution, and she insists on owning the former. She welcomes expert assistance on coding, design, and wording, but within constraints set by her conceptual goals and quality bar. This means she treats collaborators and tools as powerful implementers of her vision rather than co-authors of her theoretical stance. Effective support respects her conceptual leadership while offering concrete, high-skill help on realization.
1.context: This insight is most relevant when offering design, coding, or writing help on Dora’s projects. It should guide collaborators to ask about and defer to her conceptual framework, while proactively proposing concrete implementations, refactors, or phrasings that fit her defined aims.
supporting_evidence: Dora provides her own LaTeX-formulated theory about Alexandria vs. Schwartz and then specifically asks how to implement correlation matrices, trajectory plots, and trade-off detection in Python/pandas, clearly separating conceptual model from technical execution (ID 115).

She frames Claude as “an expert designer in modern Mac OS apps” and then incrementally constrains the design brief—seeking more aesthetic coherence, moving styles to CSS, and preserving the macOS notification look but making it more readable—showing that she sets the design direction while delegating the implementation (IDs 118–125).

In writing, Dora asks for help with micro-tasks like “help me finish this sentence,” “help me refine this paragraph,” and “add something at the end that is a little more forward-looking,” but she herself defines the conceptual goals of the HCLLMs paper, indicating that she retains control over argument and framing (IDs 130–133, 136–137).

1.title: Autonomy-Seeking, Socially Cautious Support Use
1.tagline: Dora strives to solve problems independently and hesitates to burden human mentors, so she leans heavily on AI tools for support.
1.insight: Dora’s default stance is to take responsibility for figuring things out on her own, especially with respect to busy senior researchers. At the same time, she still needs feedback and help, and she channels a large share of these needs into interactions with AI tools. This combination allows her to access deep, low-cost assistance without the social friction of asking human authority figures. It also raises her expectations for AI as a responsive, expert collaborator she can consult frequently without guilt.
1.context: This insight is most relevant when considering how Dora will seek help and how to design support structures for her (e.g., lab processes, AI tools). It suggests that self-serve, always-available, high-quality assistance will be heavily used, while synchronous or high-cost human support may be underutilized unless explicitly invited.
supporting_evidence: Dora says she almost never reaches out to postdocs or faculty because they’re “really busy” and it’s her job “to figure things out on [her] own,” only asking when someone “knows 100%” and she “knows 0%,” indicating a strong reluctance to impose on senior people (ID 143).

She asks Claude to help craft a deferential Slack message to advisors about a Google recruiter, carefully wording it around “wanted to see if it makes sense for me to interview / how I should think through this” and suggesting they can “chat during the 1-1 tomorrow,” highlighting her concern with not overstepping or demanding time (IDs 144–145).

Dora uses Claude heavily for debugging, wording, and design work—persisting when things are “still not working”—rather than immediately turning to lab mates or advisors, showing a pattern of offloading micro-decisions and stuck points to an always-available AI assistant (IDs 138–139, 130–133, 118–123).

1.title: Integrative, Multidimensional View of Quality
1.tagline: For Dora, good research requires aligned rigor across theory, methods, visuals, and prose; weaknesses in any dimension feel unacceptable.
1.insight: Dora treats conceptual soundness, statistical correctness, visual coherence, and clear writing as jointly necessary components of quality. She does not view these as separate or optional layers, but as intertwined aspects of doing serious research. As a result, support that improves one dimension while neglecting others can still feel unsatisfactory to her. Effective collaboration should aim for integrative improvements that respect all four dimensions.
1.context: This insight applies when scoping help for Dora’s projects—whether offering analysis plans, visualization templates, or writing edits. It suggests that proposals should anticipate methodological, visual, and narrative implications together, rather than optimizing only one aspect (e.g., stats without readability).
supporting_evidence: Conceptually and statistically, Dora asks for “strong, defensible evidence” that fits her cognitive claim about value tensions, not just fit-to-theory correlations, and she carefully differentiates between appropriate tests for different data types and setups, showing high standards on both theory and methods (IDs 110–113, 115–117, 126–129).

Visually, she rejects designs where “button colors don’t really make sense,” insists on separating styles into CSS for structure, and prioritizes text readability over visual effects like blurred backgrounds, demonstrating that aesthetics, structure, and usability are all important to her (IDs 118–123, 120–121).

In writing, Dora iteratively improves clarity, structure, and forward-looking framing in her HCLLMs paper, not just grammatical polish, indicating that narrative coherence and conceptual positioning are central to her sense of quality (IDs 130–133, 135–137).

1.title: Anxious but Methodologically Self-Critical
1.tagline: Dora experiences significant anxiety about methodological criticism yet uses it as a springboard for deeper understanding rather than mere damage control.
1.insight: Dora is both emotionally anxious and rigorously self-critical when facing methodological critique. She worries not only about review outcomes but also about whether her filtering decisions are genuinely justifiable. Despite this anxiety, she engages deeply with methodological questions, revising explanations and interrogating citations instead of accepting superficial fixes. Her stance combines emotional vulnerability with a strong commitment to intellectual honesty and learning.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is responding to peer reviews, revising methods sections, or making decisions about data processing and statistical justification in scholarly work.
supporting_evidence: Dora is described as “somewhat anxious about how to respond to the methodological criticism” and wants to handle the 2AC review “carefully and correctly,” showing emotional strain around methodological feedback while still focusing on proper handling of it (148).

She worries that the filtering itself “isn't justifiable,” asks “are you being honest with me,” and feels “uncertain or lack of confidence about statistical terminology,” indicating self-doubt that goes beyond simple concern over an accept/reject decision (179, 185, 152).

At the same time, Dora is “motivated to improve the paper’s methodological justification” and is “intellectually engaged with the critique,” repeatedly revising explanations of data filtering and asking Claude to “be critical” rather than simply validating her choices (150, 176, 177).

She questions whether Maas & Hox (2005) truly support her claims and refuses to make false claims about standard practice, and she asks for “conceptual clarification of ‘a priori vs post-hoc filtering’,” signaling a desire for genuine understanding, not just appeasing reviewers (177, 181, 149, 151, 153).

1.title: Persistent Executor, Externally Guided Diagnostician
1.tagline: Dora is methodical and persistent in executing problem-solving steps but tends to outsource technical diagnosis and interpretation to external helpers.
1.insight: In technical debugging, Dora shows high perseverance and careful data collection while relying on others for interpretive work. She systematically gathers and forwards detailed error messages rather than abandoning the task when issues arise. However, she rarely hypothesizes about root causes herself, instead waiting for Claude to interpret logs and propose solutions. This reflects a problem-solving style that is thorough in execution but externally guided in analysis.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is working on complex technical tasks like debugging software, configuring tools, or resolving runtime errors where she can collect evidence but prefers expert guidance for interpretation.
supporting_evidence: Dora “appears determined and methodical, actively debugging rather than giving up when things break,” and each time the extension fails she “quickly copies the exact console stack traces or error lines and sends them,” demonstrating persistence and systematic behavior (156, 157).

She surfaces multiple detailed errors—such as a React hydration error, Content Security Policy (CSP) issues, and `web_accessible_resources` denial—from different sites and keeps iterating, showing careful data gathering and continued engagement (155, 157).

The description notes that Dora seems “mildly confused or uncertain about the root cause and is relying on Claude as a debugging assistant,” indicating she does not feel confident in her own diagnosis (158).

She “does not speculate about the cause herself, but instead posts raw errors … and waits for Claude to interpret them and propose updated `manifest.json` and injection strategies,” highlighting her preference to delegate interpretation while she executes the suggested steps (159).

1.title: Tight Structural Control with Targeted Conceptual Help
1.tagline: Dora tightly controls structure, space, and style in her writing while selectively inviting deep conceptual refinement.
1.insight: In writing, Dora maintains firm control over structure, length, and stylistic consistency, even when collaborating. She enforces constraints like keeping content to a single paragraph and aligning with CHI/UIST styles, while preserving LaTeX citation syntax and academic scaffolding. Yet she actively seeks help on substantive conceptual issues, such as where to expand arguments and how to complete nuanced sentences. Collaboration is therefore bounded: she welcomes conceptual enrichment but insists on retaining her own structure and voice.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is drafting or revising academic prose under strict venue or word-limit constraints, especially when working with collaborators or tools that suggest text edits.
supporting_evidence: Dora asks to “add to my paragraph” but insists on keeping it “to one paragraph” even after being offered a two-paragraph option, indicating strong structural constraints and control (161, 163).

She is described as “time-conscious and efficiency-oriented,” and she provides style examples from CHI and UIST, asking Claude to match them, showing her focus on venue-appropriate style and fixed formatting expectations (162, 170, 171, 181).

Dora maintains LaTeX citation syntax and placeholders, indicating that she is attentive to “proper academic scaffolding rather than just prose polish” and wants the generated text to integrate seamlessly into her existing scholarly structure (164, 165).

At the same time, she explicitly asks for “areas to expand” and “not just tweak wording,” and she asks Claude to “finish this last part” of a nuanced sentence while evaluating multiple conceptual options to see what best fits her argument, demonstrating a desire for substantive conceptual refinement within her self-imposed constraints (161, 160, 165–167, 168, 169).

1.title: Selective Delegation: Routine Tasks vs. Scholarly Validity
1.tagline: Dora readily delegates low-stakes formatting work but maintains meticulous control over anything affecting methodological rigor or reviewer perception.
1.insight: Dora differentiates sharply between routine tasks and those tied to scholarly validity. For low-stakes activities like formatting, she issues terse instructions and appears comfortable accepting outputs with minimal oversight. In contrast, she exerts strong scrutiny over content that shapes how her methodology is perceived, meticulously revising phrasing and correcting misunderstandings. This pattern shows a strategic allocation of attention: efficiency for mechanics, rigor and control for interpretive and methodological claims.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is preparing rebuttals, revising papers for specific venues, or splitting work between herself and tools or collaborators, especially around formatting versus methodological explanation.
supporting_evidence: For formatting-like work, Dora gives terse instructions such as “Turn this table review into the UIST style I have provided,” then posts “Table Review:” and “UIST style:” with no extra explanation, suggesting she expects the assistant to handle style conversion autonomously (170, 171).

She likely wants to “minimize rework and cognitive load” by having Claude match the existing UIST rebuttal style exactly, and the narrative notes that she accepts the produced narrative without further edits, implying low perceived risk for this task (174, 175).

In contrast, she is “strategically focused on addressing critiques thoroughly but concisely,” constantly checking whether her responses “answer the question” and adjusting length to stay within rebuttal constraints, which shows active management of reviewer-facing content (182, 183).

Dora corrects Claude’s misunderstanding about the ≥10 threshold (subreddits vs. number of groups), refines single sentences about Maas & Hox, and pushes back on wording that could overclaim about convergence or standard practice, highlighting her “intellectually cautious” stance and her determination to avoid any misleading methodological claims (181, 176, 177).

1.title: Seeking Evidence-Based Reassurance
1.tagline: Dora wants emotional reassurance but only accepts it when it is grounded in careful critique and external evidence.
1.insight: Dora simultaneously seeks emotional support and rigorous, honest feedback about her work. She explicitly voices anxiety and asks whether Claude is being honest, indicating a need for reassurance about both her decisions and the helper’s trustworthiness. Yet she resists empty comfort, instructing Claude to “be critical” and rejecting reassurances that rely on unsupported claims. She tests the validity of comforting statements by probing the literature and external tools, accepting reassurance only when it is evidence-based.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is under evaluative pressure—such as dealing with critical reviews or methodological doubts—and is interacting with collaborators, mentors, or AI tools whose feedback she must both trust and scrutinize.
supporting_evidence: Dora expresses that she feels anxious and asks “are you being honest with me,” signaling both emotional vulnerability and a desire for honest reassurance rather than platitudes (179, 185).

She is portrayed as concerned about how her work will be perceived by reviewers, worrying not only about acceptance but also about the legitimacy of her methodological decisions, which heightens her need for reassurance that is substantively grounded (178, 182).

After being told her response is good, Dora tells Claude to “be critical,” and she rejects easy reassurances that lean on vague claims about standard practice or model behavior, insisting on accurate characterization of the `lme4`/multilevel literature (184, 177, 185).

She probes for external validation by checking Maas & Hox (2005), using the `performance` R package, and looking for citations supporting filtering of sparse groups, demonstrating that she only accepts reassurance when it is corroborated by concrete evidence and methodological authority (179).

1.title: AI as a Meta-Methodological Partner
1.tagline: Dora uses AI not just to get answers, but to design, inspect, and refine the methods and prompts that produce those answers.
1.insight: Dora treats AI as a meta-level collaborator that helps her build and iterate on reusable reasoning structures. She is less interested in single outputs and more interested in the prompts and processes that generate them. She explicitly aims to codify effective interaction patterns into systematic tools other designers can use. This positions AI as a methodological environment rather than a simple assistant.
1.context: This insight applies when designing tools, workflows, or collaborations that involve AI in research, writing, or design processes—especially where prompt engineering and reasoning frameworks are central.
supporting_evidence: Dora asks not just for good answers but for the underlying prompts and reasoning, explicitly requesting that the system “provide the prompt to produce this” (IDs 195, 197).
She defines and revises multi-step reasoning frameworks for needs synthesis, including Python-style guideline blocks and numbered “Step-by-Step Reasoning Process” stages (IDs 215, 219).

She expresses a desire to codify patterns she likes into reusable prompts for future work (ID 196) and wants to refine the prompt “as a design tool” so it becomes systematically useful for designers (ID 214).

1.title: Tension Between Bold Framing and Fear of Judgment
1.tagline: Dora wants strong, opinionated positions in her work but is simultaneously anxious about how those positions will be evaluated by others.
1.insight: Dora seeks high-conviction, designerly framings rather than neutral summaries, pushing for language that takes a clear stance. At the same time, she is worried about how reviewers and others might judge her methodological and interpretive choices. This creates a tension between her desire for boldness and her instinct for self-protection. Support that helps her express strong positions while managing perceived risk will align with this dynamic.
1.context: This insight applies when helping Dora craft arguments, write papers, frame findings, or make design/theory claims that will be visible to critical audiences (e.g., reviewers, collaborators).
supporting_evidence: Dora pushes against neutral restatements and asks for “more designerly opinion,” insisting that the `core_need` “needs to take more of a stance” (IDs 194, 195, 199, 201).
Simultaneously, she worries that reviewers will “get mad,” repeatedly asks the AI to “be honest,” and feels conflicted about revealing weaker or nonsignificant results (IDs 202, 203, 206, 207).

She recognizes fragile interactions such as effects losing significance in unfiltered models, but resists fully foregrounding that weakness, preferring a more strategic, less vulnerable presentation of those results (IDs 206, 207).

1.title: Micro-Tactical Workflow Under Cognitive Load
1.tagline: Dora manages overload by breaking complex conceptual work into many small, tightly scoped requests and edits.
1.insight: Dora is both cognitively overloaded and highly organized, using very granular tasks to stay in control of complex projects. She shifts rapidly across micro-tasks—like sentence fixes, transitions, citations, and LaTeX comments—while maintaining an overarching structure. She actively seeks scaffolds such as [INSERT] tags and precise phrasings to reduce cognitive burden in the moment. Tools that fit into this micro-tactical style while preserving the big-picture narrative will serve her best.
1.context: This insight applies when designing writing aids, editing workflows, or project-management support for Dora, particularly during intensive drafting or revision phases.
supporting_evidence: Dora is described as “cognitively overloaded but highly focused” while juggling principles vs. challenges vs. human–LLM relationships (IDs 188, 204).

Within a short period she jumps across micro-tasks like intro sentences, transitions, filling in ‘X’ in a sentence, addressing LaTeX comments, and drafting subsubsection introductions, all while tracking multiple citations (ID 189).
She explicitly asks for scaffolds such as [INSERT] tags, sentence-level transitions, and specific phrasings to support her writing and organization (IDs 205, 211).

1.title: Demand for Conceptual and Categorical Clarity
1.tagline: Dora insists on clean distinctions between types of concepts, such as needs vs. descriptions and principles vs. challenges.
1.insight: Dora places high value on conceptual hygiene and dislikes when categories are blurred or conflated. She wants clear separations between what is currently observed, what is needed or desired, and what are strategies or symptoms. Her refinement of frameworks and language reflects a commitment to systematic, well-labeled structures. Interactions that mirror and reinforce these distinctions will feel precise and trustworthy to her.
1.context: This insight applies when structuring frameworks, taxonomies, outlines, or theoretical sections in Dora’s writing or design work, as well as when translating empirical findings into design needs.
supporting_evidence: Dora focuses on how to organize “principles versus challenges” in a chapter and cares about clear signposting of conceptual framing (IDs 188, 190, 192, 193).

She flags confusion between observations of current behavior and future-oriented needs in “trusted-expert status” feedback, asking explicitly for updated reasoning and examples to separate these (IDs 212, 213, 217).
She refines guidance to distinguish observations, strategies, symptoms, and underlying needs in her frameworks, pushing for sharper categorical boundaries (IDs 215, 219).

1.title: Perfectionism as Methodological Rigor
1.tagline: Dora’s perfectionism manifests as intense attention to methodological precision and language, which both slows and strengthens her work.
1.insight: Dora persistently fine-tunes methodological descriptions and terminology, indicating a deep commitment to rigor. She is careful about how she labels statistical quantities and insists on accurate, non-hallucinated citations. At the same time, she worries about length and clarity, asking for help to streamline without losing substance. This combination means she benefits from support that preserves technical accuracy while reducing verbosity and cognitive strain.
1.context: This insight applies when assisting Dora with methods sections, technical writing, literature reviews, or any context requiring precise statistical or methodological communication.
supporting_evidence: Dora repeatedly refines methodological paragraphs about Prolific recruitment, mixed-effects modeling, and Monte Carlo power analysis at a granular level, including debating whether to call β an “effect size” (IDs 205, 208, 209).

She explicitly seeks non-hallucinated citations for niche methodological points and wants to summarize specific works like Bell, Ferron, & Kromrey (2008) accurately, rather than accepting generic references (ID 205).
She is self-critical about length and clarity, asking to “make it less long” and to improve sentence connections and scaffolding to keep the text both rigorous and readable (IDs 210, 211).

1.title: Workarounds and Invisible Work as Expertise
1.tagline: Dora sees her workaround behaviors and invisible organizational labor as legitimate expertise that should be recognized and sometimes amplified, not erased.
1.insight: Dora identifies strongly with the idea that her adaptive, behind-the-scenes work constitutes real expertise. She does not want tools or framings that treat these behaviors solely as problems to be fixed. Instead, she wants explicit consideration of when to amplify versus mitigate such strategies. Tools and insights that honor, surface, and strategically leverage her workaround practices will feel more aligned with her self-concept and goals.
1.context: This insight applies when framing user behaviors in research outputs, designing supportive tools or workflows, or discussing how to improve Dora’s (or similar users’) practices without invalidating their existing expertise.
supporting_evidence: Dora resonates with the stance that her workaround behavior is a form of expertise and accepts the characterization of her doing “invisible organizational work,” indicating she sees value and legitimacy in these activities (IDs 200, 201).

She asks for the `core_need` language to explicitly take a stance on whether to “amplify or mitigate these behaviors,” rather than framing them as neutral or purely negative issues to eliminate (ID 199).
This shows that Dora wants her adaptive strategies to be seen, respected, and sometimes strengthened, not pathologized or erased by design interventions or theoretical framings.

2.title: Integrated Rigor Across Theory, Implementation, and Presentation
2.tagline: Dora sees theory, code, metrics, writing, and visuals as one coherent system that all must meet a high bar of rigor and maintainability.
2.insight: Across her work, Dora treats conceptual framing, statistical methods, implementation details, and communication as tightly coupled layers of a single system. She expects the same level of care for architectural design, CSS separation, metrics definitions, and prose clarity, and she resists partial fixes that improve only one dimension. This produces integrative solutions where conceptual soundness, technical correctness, and communicative clarity are jointly optimized. It also means she finds support unsatisfying if it is narrowly focused on just code, just stats, or just wording without considering cross-layer implications.
2.context: Applies when scoping collaboration on research, data pipelines, UI systems, or papers—especially when deciding how much to invest in architecture, methods, and narrative coherence at once.
2.supporting_evidence: Draws on her equal attention to theory and implementation (1), preference for standard, future-proof architecture (3), movement between theory and execution (4), dual focus on real-world utility and academic rigor (8), insistence on cognitive claims from behavior (15), perfectionist standards (16, 29), integrative view of quality (19), and demand for conceptual and categorical clarity (28).

2.title: Perfectionism as a Double-Edged Methodological Engine
2.tagline: Dora’s perfectionism drives high-quality, precise work but also generates stress, time pressure, and fear of criticism.
2.insight: Dora holds herself to exacting standards across methods, language, visuals, and framing, repeatedly tightening definitions, labels, and operationalizations as she uncovers edge cases or ambiguities. This perfectionism fuels deep rigor and careful methodological nuance but also leads to visible self-critique and reluctance to accept “good enough” solutions. She worries about overclaiming, mislabeling, or being vulnerable to valid critique, which can slow decisions and heighten anxiety under deadlines. Effective collaboration must respect her need for precision while gently negotiating scope and sufficiency to avoid burnout.
2.context: Applies when setting timelines, negotiating tradeoffs, or revising analyses and papers—especially close to submission deadlines or critical reviews.
2.supporting_evidence: Synthesizes her iterative tightening of technical requirements (6), high standards and perfectionism (16, 29), anxious but methodologically self-critical stance (20), need for evidence-based reassurance (24), and tension between bold framing and fear of judgment (26).

2.title: End-to-End Conceptual Ownership with Targeted Execution Support
2.tagline: Dora insists on owning the conceptual core of her work while actively delegating and collaborating on execution details.
2.insight: Dora draws a sharp boundary between high-level theoretical framing and lower-level execution, claiming clear authorship over the former. She welcomes help with coding, formatting, wording, and micro-level decisions, but always within constraints defined by her conceptual goals and standards. In debugging and writing, she performs persistent execution work and careful evidence collection while leaning on external partners for specialized interpretation or packaging. This division of labor positions her as the architect and owner of the system’s meaning, with others acting as expert implementers and refiners.
2.context: Applies when working with her on code, design, or writing—e.g., as a collaborator, advisor, or tool designer who must respect her conceptual leadership while contributing concrete solutions.
2.supporting_evidence: Combines her end-to-end ownership with selective gaps in platform mechanics (5), stance as concept owner/execution collaborator (17), persistence in debugging but reliance on external diagnosis (21), tight structural control in writing (22), and selective delegation between routine and validity-critical tasks (23).

2.title: Micro-Tactical Workflows Under High Cognitive Load
2.tagline: Dora manages heavy cognitive and emotional load by breaking work into granular, well-structured micro-tasks while keeping a clear big-picture arc.
2.insight: When cognitively overloaded, Dora organizes her work into many small, tightly specified tasks—sentence-level fixes, transitions, citations, configuration tweaks—anchored by a clear overarching structure. She uses scaffolds like [INSERT] tags, modular sections, and precise constraints on length and style to offload moment-to-moment decision-making. This micro-tactical style allows her to keep momentum in complex projects without losing global coherence, but it also depends on tools and collaborators respecting her structural plan. The approach is both a productivity strategy and a way to mitigate overwhelm.
2.context: Applies during intensive drafting, last-minute revisions, or multi-component system building where she is juggling many interdependent elements under time or emotional pressure.
2.supporting_evidence: Inferred from her fast oscillation between theory and execution (4), delegation of micro-writing tasks (12), structural control in text (22), separation of routine vs. scholarly tasks (23), and micro-tactical workflow under cognitive load (27).

2.title: Emotional Ergonomics as a First-Class Design Constraint
2.tagline: Dora treats users’ emotional experiences—calm, pressure, surveillance, agency—as equally important to technical correctness and performance.
2.insight: In her design decisions, Dora systematically evaluates how elements like timers, colors, wording, and framing shape users’ emotional states, not just task completion. She pushes back against purely functional or abstract technical advice, insisting that solutions must be grounded in real lived contexts and avoid triggering unnecessary anxiety or pressure. She uses the AI to probe how subtle framing shifts may alter users’ feelings of control, comparison, or safety, effectively treating emotional ergonomics as a design variable. This leads her to prioritize interfaces that are technically sound while being emotionally non-intrusive and supportive.
2.context: Applies when making UX decisions, writing interface copy, or choosing interaction patterns that will be used by people under stress, evaluation, or vulnerability.
2.supporting_evidence: Grounded in her attention to emotional impact in UX (0) and her systematic use of AI to compare framings for user emotions and experience (13).

2.title: Structuring Complexity and Emotion Through Systems and Labels
2.tagline: Dora turns messy, emotionally charged domains into structured, labeled systems to gain both cognitive clarity and emotional distance.
2.insight: In both technical and personal contexts, Dora manages complexity by decomposing it into modules, roles, and labeled components. She does this with advisor relationships, impostor feelings, and review responsibilities, as well as in software and methodological design. This structuralization acts as an emotional buffer: by placing difficult issues into frameworks, processes, and named categories, she can engage with them analytically rather than being overwhelmed. The same impulse underlies her desire for clear taxonomies and categorical hygiene in her theoretical work.
2.context: Applies when she is navigating high-stakes interpersonal situations, self-reflection, or large, complex projects where emotional and technical stakes are intertwined.
2.supporting_evidence: Synthesizes her use of structure and modularization to manage emotion (7), third-person meta-analysis as emotional buffer (9), and strong demand for conceptual and categorical clarity (28).

2.title: Third-Person Framing and Calibration of Self-Descriptions
2.tagline: Dora uses third-person and meta-level language about herself to explore vulnerabilities safely and avoid over-pathologizing.
2.insight: Dora frequently refers to herself in the third person and explicitly experiments with different descriptive framings of “Dora,” comparing how they feel and what they imply. She treats psychological labels and narratives as adjustable in strength, contrasting more diagnostic language with more neutral or operational phrasing. This practice helps her examine issues like anxiety and impostor syndrome with some emotional distance while resisting narratives that reduce her to a diagnosis. She seeks a balance where language is accurate and nuanced but does not fix her identity in rigid, clinical terms.
2.context: Applies during coaching-like conversations, reflective writing, or any attempt to characterize her psychological patterns or motivations.
2.supporting_evidence: Emerges from her habit of talking about herself in the third person as a protective strategy (9) and her active calibration of diagnostic versus neutral psychological language (10).

2.title: Authority–Self-Doubt Tension Resolved Through Teaching Frames
2.tagline: Dora is uneasy occupying pure authority roles and reframes evaluation as collaborative teaching to feel legitimate.
2.insight: When asked to act as a reviewer, mentor, or validator, Dora experiences tension between her high standards and doubts about her right to judge others. To manage this, she actively reframes evaluative tasks as opportunities to teach, share expertise, or collaboratively improve work rather than to pass judgment. This shift reduces anxiety and aligns better with her self-concept as a knowledgeable collaborator rather than a gatekeeper. Her recurring attention to themes like “seeking validation while being the validator” indicates this conflict is ongoing and central.
2.context: Applies when assigning her formal evaluative responsibilities—peer review, mentoring, grading—or asking her to provide critical feedback to others.
2.supporting_evidence: Based on her explicit reframing of evaluation as teaching to reduce authority anxiety (14) combined with the broader tension between bold framing and fear of judgment (26).

2.title: Collaborative Rigor Over Praise: How Dora Uses Feedback
2.tagline: Dora prioritizes adversarial, evidence-based critique over reassurance and expects collaborators to act as critical co-thinkers.
2.insight: In interactions with AI and likely humans, Dora explicitly steers away from praise and toward rigorous stress-testing of her ideas. She repeatedly asks for steel-manned counterarguments and detailed failure modes instead of comfort, while still needing to trust that feedback is honest and grounded. Reassurance is acceptable only when backed by solid evidence, citations, or clear methodological reasoning. This means the most helpful collaborators will challenge her constructively, scrutinize claims, and avoid empty encouragement that she cannot verify.
2.context: Applies when giving her feedback on research, design, or writing, especially under evaluative pressure or during rebuttals and revisions.
2.supporting_evidence: Draws on her use of external help for validation and refinement rather than instruction (2), preference for rigorous critique over reassurance (11), anxious but methodologically self-critical stance (20), and seeking evidence-based reassurance (24).

2.title: Autonomy-Seeking Use of Low-Cost, High-Expertise Support
2.tagline: Dora prefers to solve problems independently but leans heavily on AI as a low-friction, expert collaborator.
2.insight: Dora is socially cautious about burdening busy senior researchers and defaults to taking responsibility for figuring things out herself. To bridge the gap between her need for feedback and her reluctance to ask humans, she channels many support needs into interactions with AI tools she can query frequently without guilt. This raises her expectations for responsiveness, expertise, and honesty from AI-based collaborators, which she treats as always-available co-thinkers. It also means that if human support structures are not explicitly invited and made low-friction, she may underuse them.
2.context: Applies when designing mentorship structures, lab processes, or AI-based tools for her, and when interpreting her help-seeking behavior.
2.supporting_evidence: Integrates her use of AI for competent validation (2), autonomy-seeking yet socially cautious support use (18), externally guided diagnosis style in debugging (21), and view of AI as a meta-methodological partner (25).

2.title: AI as Meta-Methodological Environment and Mirror
2.tagline: Dora uses AI not just for answers, but to design, test, and codify reusable reasoning and interaction structures.
2.insight: Beyond single outputs, Dora is interested in the prompts, patterns, and workflows that generate good reasoning and user experiences. She uses AI to prototype framing variations, compare their emotional and cognitive effects, and then abstract these into tools and methods other designers could reuse. The AI becomes a testbed for interaction designs, a mirror for her own identity framings, and a sandbox for meta-methodological refinement. This positions AI as an environment for building reusable cognitive and design infrastructure rather than a simple Q&A assistant.
2.context: Applies when collaborating on prompt engineering, tool design, or research methods that involve AI, and when she asks for not just solutions but reusable templates.
2.supporting_evidence: Combines her use of AI as a mirror and design probe for framing effects (13) with her explicit treatment of AI as a meta-methodological partner for building reusable reasoning structures (25).

2.title: Behavioral Traces as Windows into Underlying Cognitive Tensions
2.tagline: Dora aims to infer structured reasoning and value tensions from observable behavior, not just describe patterns.
2.insight: In her analytic work, Dora is dissatisfied with results that merely report associations or validate existing frameworks. She seeks methods that allow strong claims about how people navigate tensions, deliberate, and prioritize among values based on their behavioral traces. This drives her toward more sophisticated tests, higher evidentiary standards, and designs that can distinguish between different underlying cognitive processes. Her bar for interpretive validity is thus higher than simple descriptive statistics or surface-level behavioral summaries.
2.context: Applies when designing experiments, defining metrics, or choosing modeling approaches in projects about decision-making, values, or cognitive processes.
2.supporting_evidence: Directly grounded in her stated desire for methods that support robust cognitive and value-tension claims from behavior rather than static associations (15).

2.title: Emotional Vulnerability Coupled with Intellectual Honesty
2.tagline: Dora’s anxiety about evaluation coexists with a strong commitment to methodological learning and self-correction.
2.insight: When facing critique, Dora is emotionally vulnerable and worries about both outcomes and the justifiability of her decisions. Yet instead of defensiveness or superficial fixes, she engages deeply with methodological concerns, revising explanations, interrogating citations, and updating her reasoning. She also demands that comfort be grounded in real evidence rather than platitudes. This combination makes her responsive to high-quality critique while also needing careful, non-dismissing handling of her anxiety.
2.context: Applies during peer review responses, high-stakes feedback sessions, or mentoring conversations about her methods and decisions.
2.supporting_evidence: Synthesizes her anxious but methodologically self-critical responses to critique (20) and her insistence on evidence-based reassurance (24).

2.title: Workarounds and Invisible Labor as Legitimate Expertise
2.tagline: Dora views her adaptive, behind-the-scenes work not as pathology but as a form of skilled expertise that tools should respect and sometimes amplify.
2.insight: Dora resonates with the idea that her invisible, compensatory, or workaround behaviors—like structuring messy domains or juggling micro-tasks—are expressions of real competence. She resists problem framings that treat these strategies only as things to eliminate, preferring approaches that distinguish between harmful friction and adaptive skill. She wants tools and analyses that explicitly surface, honor, and strategically leverage such practices, while carefully deciding when mitigation is actually beneficial. This stance shapes how she wants both her own and users’ behaviors to be represented and supported.
2.context: Applies when framing user behavior in research outputs, designing supportive tools, or discussing changes to her own workflows and coping strategies.
2.supporting_evidence: Combines her use of structure to cope with complexity (7), micro-tactical workflows under load (27), and explicit identification of workarounds and invisible work as expertise (30).

2.title: Selective Delegation to Preserve Cognitive Bandwidth
2.tagline: Dora offloads routine and micro-level tasks so she can focus her limited bandwidth on high-stakes conceptual and methodological decisions.
2.insight: Dora strategically delegates formatting, phrasing, and other routine tasks with minimal oversight while tightly controlling anything that bears on scholarly validity or conceptual framing. She treats AI as a micro-decision assistant for language and structure, issuing constrained instructions that preserve her own ideas and voice. This allows her to invest attention where it matters most—methods, framing, and interpretive claims—while maintaining productivity on lower-stakes work. It also means she expects collaborators to clearly differentiate between mechanical and substantive changes.
2.context: Applies when assisting her with drafting, revising, or preparing documents for specific venues, as well as when splitting work in collaborations.
2.supporting_evidence: Rooted in her pattern of delegating micro-writing to preserve bandwidth (12) and her selective attention allocation between routine tasks and scholarly validity (23).

2.title: Standards-Driven, Future-Proof Architectural Thinking
2.tagline: Dora resists one-off hacks and prefers solutions grounded in standards, interoperability, and a clear long-term architecture.
2.insight: When choosing technologies or designing systems, Dora looks first for de facto standards and patterns that will age well rather than quick or idiosyncratic fixes. She often requests high-level conceptual overviews before committing to specific implementations, and she challenges premature framework choices if their rationale is weak. This forward-looking stance reflects her desire for systems that remain understandable, maintainable, and interoperable across components and over time. She treats ad-hoc shortcuts as acceptable only when clearly justified and contained.
2.context: Applies when discussing tech stacks, configuration systems, or multi-component architectures, and when deciding between speed and long-term robustness.
2.supporting_evidence: Draws from her equal rigor for theory and implementation (1), explicit preference for standards and future-proofing (3), and desire for coherent end-to-end pipelines (5).
